examples.title: NRZ Encoding Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to implement Non-Return-to-Zero line encoding
examples.code:
ALGORITHM NRZ_Encode(data)
BEGIN
 FOR each bit in data DO
 IF bit = 1 THEN
 output HIGH voltage
 ELSE
 output LOW voltage (0V)
 END IF
 wait for bit duration
 END FOR
END

// Example: Data 10110
// Output: HIGH, LOW, HIGH, HIGH, LOW
// Signal stays at level for entire bit period
examples.explanation: NRZ encodes binary 1 as high voltage and 0 as low voltage for the entire bit duration. Simple but may lose synchronization in long runs of same bits.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of bits

examples.title: Manchester Encoding Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm for Manchester line encoding used in Ethernet
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Manchester_Encode(data)
BEGIN
 FOR each bit in data DO
 IF bit = 1 THEN
 // Positive to negative transition at mid-bit
 output HIGH for half bit time
 output LOW for half bit time
 ELSE
 // Negative to positive transition at mid-bit
 output LOW for half bit time
 output HIGH for half bit time
 END IF
 END FOR
END

// Example: Data 1011
// Output: ↑↓ ↑↓ ↑↓ ↑↓
// Each bit has guaranteed transition for clock sync
examples.explanation: Manchester encoding ensures a transition in every bit period providing clock synchronization. Used in 10 Mbps Ethernet. Double bandwidth required compared to NRZ.

examples.title: Calculate Channel Capacity (Shannon)
examples.description: Algorithm to compute maximum channel capacity
examples.explanation: Shannon's theorem gives theoretical maximum data rate for a noisy channel. Real systems achieve lower rates due to practical limitations.

examples.title: Calculate Nyquist Maximum Data Rate
examples.description: Algorithm to compute maximum data rate for noiseless channel
examples.explanation: Nyquist theorem applies to noiseless channels. It shows that increasing signal levels (M) increases data rate but makes signal detection harder in practice.
